TELEFACSIMTLE (2131 626.6955
TELEX 261E23 MOFO UR

VIA FEDERAL EXPRESS
DIRECT DIAL NUMBER
(2L3) 62L-9577
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, Caflfornia 
958o4-0807
Attention: 
John wafface, Esq.
Re: Letter No. 91- 118
Dear Mr. WaIlace:
This letter is in reference to Letter No. 91-118
(the ,rRequest,r), a request for informal assistance, dated
February 22, !991, submitted by Robert K' olsen of our firm
to Mark T. Morodomi, an attorney with the commission. This
Ietter follows telephone discussions r.re have had with you
and Mr. Iuorodomi since subrnitting the Request.
As you know, the Request describes the situation of
a client of this firn (the "conpany" ) which occasionalfy is
hired to compute so-ca1led "special taxt' formulas under
contract with a public agency in connection with the
establishrnent of Mello-Roos Districts. 
The Request asks the
Commission's inforrnal written assistance to the effect that
the company's employees do not have an imperrnissible
conflict of interest under the PoIitical Reform Act v/hen
they perf orrn these services.
It is our understandinq, based on telephone
conversations $/ith you and Mr. Morodomi, that there is
tentative agreement vrith our analysis of the issues we
presented in the Request, although you have recently
identified an additional issue -- the question of whether
there exists an irnperrnissible "nexus," as defined in
Regulation Section L8702.1(d) -- that could present a
conflict of interest. 
From these telephone conversations,
(
i


MonusoN&Fornsmn
John wallacef Esq.
August 2, L99L
Page Tr^to
rre al-so understood that one type of situation in which the
company sornetimes finds itself raises no conflict of
interest concern. This situation, not described in the
Request, is one where the company is not involved in
preparing the developer's proposal for formation of the
district, 
but has its first 
involvement vrhen the public
agency asks the Company to evaluate the subnitted proposal.
(The situation described in the Request is one hlhere the
Company is involved at the proposal development stage as
v/eIl- as after the proposal has been submitted. )
Recently, in the course of our preliminary
discussions on the ttnexus" issue, we had the opportunity to
describe the nature of the Conpany's work in rnore detail
than set forth in the Request. Based on this description,
you suggested that we provide the Commission with a fuLfer
written description of the Company's services. !{e
understand that, depending on the facts, the companyrs
employees rnight not be "consuftants't in the performance of
the services in question, in which case they would not be
,public officials,, and no conflict of interest issue woufd
arise.
Although you said you do not want a full legal
/brieftr on the issue, we wiII note here that, in general, a
trpublic officialt' 
includes a "consultant" of a state or
Local governmental agency. Gov. Code Section A2O48. A
frconsultant,, includes "any natural person who provides under
contractf inforrnation, advice, recommendation or counsel to
a state or locaL government agencyr" but does not include a
person who (1) does so ttindependent of control and direction
of the agency or any agency official, 
other than normal
contract monitoringr't and (2) generally possesses no
,,authority with respect to any agency decision. " 
Reg.
Section 187oo(a)(2).
Employees of the company have no officiaf
dec i s ion-rnaki-ng authority with regard to the formation of a
district, 
so the second el,enent of Section 18700(a)(2) is
clearly met. we afso believe the employees provide the
conpany,s services independent of the controf and direction
of the public agency.
When the Company is hired by the public agency, it
is for the purpose of preparing a t'special taxtr f orrnula in
connection with a proposed district to be formed by the


MonrrsoN &Fornsrnn
John Wallace, Esq.
August 2, ],991
Page Three
agency. This special tax formula is used in apportioning
special tax rates anong assessor's parcels within the
district to pay debt service on bonds issued to finance
public infrastructure. 
The companyts role is essentially
that of a ,,number cruncher[i it has developed particular
computer programs and expertise in calculating how these
special taxes can be spread' based on variables provided to
the company by the agency. The agency and its team of bond
counsel, underwriters and others develop various possible
methods for the financing of the proposed district. 
As
these various nethods are developed, sets of key input
variables, such as the desired level of tax, the maturity of
each bond issue, and the timing and cost of contenplated
public irnprovements, nay be changed. As these input
variables change, they are fed to the conpany, whose
employees perforn the necessary calculations to produce new
iterations of a special tax formula. Whj.le the companyrs
services are obviously important in the over-al,l- effort to
finalize the plan for a proposed district, 
the Companyf
because it atone possesses this technical expertise, works
independently of the agency in performing the repeated
calculations required for developing the special tax
formula.
we believe these facts show that the Company's
ernployees are not acting as t'consultants" when they work
with public agencies. However, if the Commission disagrees,
then the recentfy-raised issue of whether there is a rrnexus"
witt remain. 
we have not yet had a fult opportunity to
present our analysis on this point, and would appreciate the
opportunity to discuss it with the cornmission if this natter
is not resolved on the basis of the "consultant" issue.
We look forv/ard to hearing from you.
me should you have any questions or comments.
Please cal l
very truly yours,
fu4K*,
KDD/nab
Enc Iosures

:' l lt 
r
' N'rw t'onx
"wASHrNGr?Drl D.c.
\.LOilIDON. J I
HONG KONG
TOKYO
Frn Z
February 22, 1991
VTA CERTIFIED MAIL -- RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 8O7
Sacramento, California 
95804-0807
Attention: 
Mark T. Morodomi, Esq.
Re: Reguest for Informal Assistanee
Gentlemen and Ladies:
This is a request for informal written assistance
from the Fair Political Practices Commission under
Regulation 1,8329(c).L/ We have been authorized to request
this assistance by a client whose obligations under the
Po}itical Reform Act, Government Code S 8L000 et seq. (the
,,Act,, ) , are in question.
our client (the rrcompany") is a California
corporation which from tirne to time provides special tax
consulting services under contract to loca1 government
agencies in connection with the establishment of so-caIled
u5l1o-noos Districts under the Mello-Roos community
Facilities Act of Lg82 (Government Code S 53311 et seg. )
The Company,s principal work is to prepare the methodology
L/
0f
A11 references
the catifornia
to regulations are to Title
Code of Regulations.
2, Division 6


Monnrsou &Fonnsrrn
Fair Pofitical Practices Cornnission
February 22, L99l
Page Two
for allocating (or ,tspreadingtt ) the Mello-Roos taxes over
the relevant property.2/
QUESTION
Do the conf f ict-of- interest provisions of the Act
preclude non-owner employees of the Company (,,Employees,,)
from providing tax consulting services to a public agency in
connection with the establishment of a Melfo-Roos District
where the Conpany has performed similar services for the
developer of the real property located within the proposed
Mello-Roos District within the preceding 12 nonths?
PROPOSED CONCLUS ION
The Companyts Employees do not have an
imperrnissibl-e conflict of interest under the Act when they
perform Me1fo-Roos tax consulting services for a public
agency under the circumstances described above. It is not
reasonably foreseeable, in light of the fact that the tax
consulting services required in establishing a MeLlo-Roos
District must be complete or substantiatly cornplete before
the District is approved or disapproved, that the Company
would receive any material anount of future business from
the developer in connection with the proposed District as a
result of the decision whether to establish the District.
FACTS
From tine to time, the Company provides preliminary
Mello-Roos tax consulting services on a fee basis (with fees
exceeding $250) to a land developer as part of the
devefoper,s preparation of a proposal (sometimes in
conjunction with one or more other developers ) to the
relevant public agency for the establishment of a Meflo-Roos
District. 
(?he relevant agency is the city or county having
jurisdiction over the area of the proposed District. ) In
some instances, the public agency w111 then request the
?/ The Meflo-Roos Act provides for the establishment of
special bond financing districts by local government
agencies to finance the construction of cornrnunity facilities
(for example, fire stations, libraries, schools and parks)
within the area covered by the MeIlo-Roos District. 
Id.
SS 53313, 53313.5. The bonds are repaid by special taxes
levied on the real property located within the Mello-Roos
District. 
Id. S 53358.


MonnrsoN &Fonnsrnn
Fair Political Practices Commission
February 22, l99f
Page Three
company to contract directly with the public agency to
provide additional consulting services with respect to the
same proposal in order to assist the public agency in
deciding whether to recommend establishing the District.3/
It is possible that employees of the Company $/ho worked on
the project for the developer rnay thereafter work on the
project for the public agency. However, the Company does
not rrork for the developer and the public agency with
respect to the same project simultaneously,
The conpany is privately held, and no Employee has
a 10 percent or greater ownership interest in the the
Company .
ANALYS IS
Under S 87100 of the Act, no public official 
nay
participate in making a governmental decision in which the
official 
has a financial- interest.
A. 
Public official.
You nay assume for purposes of this letter that
Employees of the Company are ,,consultants,, as defined in
Regulation 18700(a)(2) r4,hen the company is providing Me1lo-
Roos tax consulting services under contract to a public
agency. A consultant is considered to be a public official
subject to the conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the Act.
In contrast, since the Conpany is not a natural
person, the Conpany is not a consultant or a public
official. 
Regulation 18700(a).
B. 
Participation in a Gcvernrnental Decision.
You may aLso assune for purposes of this letter
that the tax consuLting services rendered by the Employees
to a public agency in connection with the establishment of a
MeLlo-Roos District constitute participation in the making
of a governmenta] decision under Regulation 18700(c)(2) (the
giving of advice without significant intervening review by
the public agency).
1/ After a public agency recommends the establishrnent of a
MeLlo-Roos District, two-thirds of the relevant electors
rnust vote to f ina1l-y approve the District before it rnay be
established. See Government Code S 53326.


MonnrsoN &Fornsrnn
Fair PoLitical Practices cornmission
February 22, 1991
Page f'our
c. 
Foreseeable Material Financial Effect.
The remaining issue thus is whether, under the Act
and applicable regufations, the Enployees have a "financial
interest,, in the Mello-Roos decision in liqht of the fact
that the Company previously performed services for one or
more relevant land developers in connection with the
pro ject .
(i) 
Sources of Income to the Employees.
Section 87103(c) of the Act provides, in
pertinent part, that a public officiaf 
has a financial-
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect
( distinguishable from its effect on the public generally) on
any source of income of $250 or more to the official 
within
the 12 rnonths prior to the time the decision is rnade. Thus,
the first question is who or what constitutes a source of
income of 9250 or nore in the preceding 12 months to the
Enployees vrhen they are providing Me11o-Roos tax consulting
services as described above?
The regular wages paid to the Employees make
the company such a source of income to Employees. In
contrast, since none of the Enployees has a 10 percent or
greater ownership interest in the company, sources of incorne
to the conpany (for example, the developer) are not sources
of income to the Employees. See Act S 82030(a); FPPC letter
to Diane Fishburn, No. A-90-386 (July 9t L99o ) (,,Fishburn
Lettertt ) at note 4.
(ii) Materiaf Financial Effect on the Companv.
Thus, the only issue rernaining with respect to
the Employees is whether a public agency,s decision
regarding a proposed I,leIlo-Roos District wilI have a
reasonabfy foreseeable material financial, effect on the
Company.
The applicable rufes for deterrnining the
materiality of the effect of a governmental decision on the
Company appear in Regulation 18702.2(g) (nateriality rules
for privately held corporations). See Act S 82o3o(a);
Fishburn Letter at 7-8. The test is entirely forward-
looking. Under the regulation, the effect of a governmental
decision is material as to a business entity if the decision
wiff result in:


Monnrsotr &FornsrEn
Fair Politicaf Practices commission
February 22 | 199L
Page Five
(a) an increase or decrease in the
entity,s gross revenues for a fiscal
year of $10,000 or morei
(b) the entity,s incurring or avoiding
additionaf expenses or reducing or
elininating existing expenses for a
fiscal year of $27500 or morei or
(c) an increase or decrease in the value
of the entiiy,s assets or liabilities
of $1o,000 or more.
The question thus is whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that a MeIlo-Roos decision will have any of the
above effects on the company.
With respect to the second and third criteria
listed above, you may assume that a public agency,s
Mello-Roos decision has no reasonably foreseeable effect on
the company,s expenses or asset or liability 
values.
With respect to the first criterion (effect on
future income), the FPPC staff considered a similar question
in the Fishburn Letter, where a private civil engineering
consulting company, cella Barr Associates (xCBAx), had an
ongolng rel,ationship with a land devefoper. CBA proposed to
prepare the trspecif ic plan.t for an undeveloped area under
contract to the relevant city. 
The area included a
significant amount of land or,rned by CBAts long-time client,
the developer. The FPPC staff found that under the
circumstances it was ,tIikely/ that the devefoper would
retain CBA for future services with respect to the property
in the planning area if the specif j.c plan (which CBA was to
prepare) approved the property for development. Fishburn
Letter at 6. The FPPC thus concluded that the CBA staff
would have an impermissible conflict of interest if, 
as
seemed 1ikeIy, the additionaf development-related work CBA
would receive fron the developer in the planning area woufd
result in an increase or decrease in gross revenues of
$10,000 or more to CBA in a fiscal year. Id. at 8.
The Companyts situation is entirely different,
due to the different nature of Melto-Roos work, and a
different conclusi.on thus is ca1led for. 
Unlike cBArs
situation, there is no future prospect of the company,s
receiving additional tax consulting work from the developer
with respect to a given project after the public agency has


MonnrsoN &FornsrEn
Fair Po]itical Practices Comrnission
February 22, l99I
Paqe Six
retained the company on the project. 
This is because the
tax consulting rrork on a MeIlo-Roos District is
substantially completed by the time the public agency
recommends the approval or disapproval of the District. 
The
tax spread methodology must be completed in advance because
i-t is part of the MeIIo-Roos proposaL vrhich the agency
approves or disapproves. As a resul-t, neither the approval
nor disapproval of a Mello-Roos District can foreseeably
result in the relevant developer,s sending additional
business to the company in connection with that District.4/
our clientrs actual experience bears this analysis
out, Over the course of several years and several dozen
projects, there has been no case in which the relevant
developer has retained the company for significant
additional work on a particular proposed Me11o-Roos District
after the time the public agency has retained the Company
directly to work on that proposed District. 
(fn a few
cases, devefopers have asked the company to prepare a short
consumer disclosure about the District after it is approved.
This work has never resulted in more than 92,500 of
additional revenue to the Company. ) Likewise (again unlike
CBA,s situation in the Fishburn Letter), the Cornpany does
not perforn qeneral civil engineering, planning or other
services that could result in additionaf income if a
MeIIo-Roos District is approved.
In light of the foregoing, under the tests
established by Regulation 18702.2(S), no Employee of the
Conpany should be deemed to have a financial- interest in the
decision by a public agency whether to establish a
Mel-Io-Roos Dj,strict, even where the Cornpany has provided
prelirninary Mello-Roos tax consulting services within the
preceding l-2 nonths to the relevant developer. As a result,
the conflict of interest provisions of the Act should not
4/ In our telephone conversation on January 9, 1991, you
asked whether the developer night retain the Conpany for
future !'rork on other MeIlo-Roos Districts. 
Although such a
possibility cani6E-Entirety be excluded, it is not
reasonably foreseeabfe (as required by the Act to create a
conflict of interest) that such future business would be
forthcomingr or that any such future business would be the
result of the decision on a prior Mello-Roos District. 
As
noted in tfre flshfurn Letter, obtaining future hrork from a
developer on an unrelated project is an uncertain
expectation. Id. at note 5.


Monrusou & Fornsrnn
Fair PoliticaI Practices Cornmission
February 22, 1991
Page Seven
preclude an Employee from acting as a consultant to the
relevant public agency in connection with the Mello-Roos
decision process under such circumstances.
Please do
in our Los Angefes
comments regarding
is ( 2l-3 ) 62L-9577 .
RKo 3 jhljad
cc3 Mark R.
i0!*
not hesitate to telephone Mark R. Burrill
office should you have any questions or
the foregoing. Mark,s direct diaf nurnber
Robert K. Olsen
Burri11, Esq.

When the Company is hired by the public agency, it
is for the purpose of preparing a t'special taxtr f orrnula in

agency. This special tax formula is used in apportioning
special tax rates anong assessor's parcels within the
district to pay debt service on bonds issued to finance
public infrastructure. 
The companyts role is essentially
that of a ,,number cruncher[i it has developed particular
computer programs and expertise in calculating how these
special taxes can be spread' based on variables provided to
the company by the agency. The agency and its team of bond
counsel, underwriters and others develop various possible
methods for the financing of the proposed district. 
As
these various nethods are developed, sets of key input
variables, such as the desired level of tax, the maturity of
each bond issue, and the timing and cost of contenplated
public irnprovements, nay be changed. As these input
variables change, they are fed to the conpany, whose
employees perforn the necessary calculations to produce new
iterations of a special tax formula. Whj.le the companyrs
services are obviously important in the over-al,l- effort to
finalize the plan for a proposed district, 
the Companyf
because it atone possesses this technical expertise, works
independently of the agency in performing the repeated
calculations required for developing the special tax
formula.
we believe these facts show that the Company's

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the conf f ict-of- interest provisions of the Act
preclude non-owner employees of the Company (,,Employees,,)
from providing tax consulting services to a public agency in
connection with the establishment of a Melfo-Roos District
where the Conpany has performed similar services for the
developer of the real property located within the proposed
Mello-Roos District within the preceding 12 nonths?
PROPOSED CONCLUS ION
The Companyts Employees do not have an
imperrnissibl-e conflict of interest under the Act when they
perform Me1fo-Roos tax consulting services for a public
agency under the circumstances described above. It is not
reasonably foreseeable, in light of the fact that the tax
consulting services required in establishing a MeLlo-Roos
District must be complete or substantiatly cornplete before
the District is approved or disapproved, that the Company
would receive any material anount of future business from
the developer in connection with the proposed District as a
result of the decision whether to establish the District.

(The situation described in the Request is one hlhere the Company is involved at the proposal development stage as v/eIl- as after the proposal has been submitted. ) Recently, in the course of our preliminary discussions on the ttnexus" issue, we had the opportunity to describe the nature of the Conpany's work in rnore detail than set forth in the Request.
